content:
First, note that since each \(a_i\) lies between 2 and 3, any integer approximation \(A_i\) must be either 2 or 3 (rounding to 1 or 4 would incur an error \(>1\), which cannot help us beat a max‐error of about 0.6).  Write

• \(d_i=a_i-2\) = “error if we choose \(A_i=2\),”  
• \(u_i=3-a_i\) = “error if we choose \(A_i=3\).”

We tabulate:

i  \(a_i\)  \(d_i=a_i-2\)  \(u_i=3-a_i\)  
1  2.56  0.56  0.44  
2  2.61  0.61  0.39  
3  2.65  0.65  0.35  
4  2.71  0.71  0.29  
5  2.79  0.79  0.21  
6  2.82  0.82  0.18  
7  2.86  0.86  0.14  

We need \(\sum_iA_i=19\).  If exactly \(k\) of the \(A_i\) are 3’s and the rest 2’s, then
\[
3k+2(7-k)=19\quad\Longrightarrow\quad k=5.
\]
So we must pick exactly five 3’s and two 2’s.  If we choose the two smallest \(d_i\) to be 2’s (namely \(i=1,2\)), then:

– On those two, the larger “down‐error” is \(d_2=0.61\).  
– On the other five (all rounded up to 3), the largest “up‐error” is \(u_3=0.35<0.61\).  

Hence the maximum error is
\[
M=\max\{d_1,d_2,\dots,u_7\} \;=\;0.61.
\]
One checks that any other choice of two 2’s forces an even larger \(d_i\), and any introduction of 1’s or 4’s drives \(M>1\).  Thus
\[
M_{\min}=0.61,
\]
and
\[
100M_{\min}=61.
\]
\boxed{61}

i  \(a_i\)  \(d_i=a_i-2\)  \(u_i=3-a_i\)  
1  2.56  0.56  0.44

3  2.65  0.65  0.35
